title: Trump Attacks Supreme Court Over IEEPA Tariff Ruling; Claims $165B in Refunds Owed
detail: President Trump publicly denounced the Supreme Court's February 20, 2026 ruling that IEEPA does not authorize tariffs, directing criticism at Justices Barrett and Gorsuch who joined the 6-3 majority. Trump claimed the ruling had effectively obligated the government to issue $165 billion in tariff refunds to importers. The outburst created legal and market uncertainty about the administration's tariff path forward, as Section 122 tariffs (reimposed February 24) were already facing potential legal challenges. CNBC reported the episode added a new dimension of unpredictability to trade policy, with markets questioning whether the Section 122 regime was politically sustainable.

title: EU Parliament Backs 'Turnberry' EU-US Trade Framework 417–154 With Sunset Clause
detail: The European Parliament voted 417 to 154 (71 abstentions) to advance a preliminary EU-US trade framework dubbed the 'Turnberry' trade deal, which would set a 15% US tariff rate on EU goods — a significant reduction from the 20% 'Liberation Day' rate — in exchange for EU market opening. Lawmakers attached a sunset clause (deal expires March 2028 unless renewed) and a sunrise clause making tariff preferences conditional on the US meeting its commitments. The broad parliamentary majority signaled political will, but final ratification still requires EU Council approval and US Congressional action. The vote reduced but did not eliminate the risk of a transatlantic trade war escalation.

title: S&P 500 Posts Fifth Consecutive Weekly Loss — Longest Streak Since 2022
detail: The S&P 500 extended its losing run to a fifth consecutive week on March 26 — the longest weekly decline streak since late 2022 — driven by rising oil prices from the Iran conflict, inflation fears, and erased expectations for Federal Reserve rate cuts. The S&P 500 fell approximately 1.7% on the day, the Nasdaq dropped 2.1%, and the 'Magnificent Seven' tech stocks lost over $330 billion in combined market capitalization. The five-week losing streak compounded recession anxiety as investors wrestled with a stagflation dynamic: a slowing economy, but still-rising inflation driven by energy prices. The drawdown was the sharpest sustained equity correction since the 2022 rate-hike cycle.

title: Fed Vice Chair Jefferson Flags Energy Prices as Key Inflation Risk at March 18 Follow-Up
detail: Multiple Federal Reserve governors delivered speeches on March 26. Vice Chair Philip Jefferson specifically addressed the effect of surging energy prices on the inflation outlook, warning that oil-driven price increases could temporarily push inflation higher and complicate the Fed's path to its 2% target. Governor Lisa Cook addressed financial stability risks. Governor Michael Barr covered the economic outlook. Governor Stephen Miran discussed balance sheet reduction prospects. The collective messaging reinforced the Fed's 'wait and see' posture established at the March 18 FOMC meeting — when the Fed held rates at 3.50–3.75% — signaling no imminent cuts given the energy-driven inflation shock.
